# A framework for thinking about the potential public health impact of epidemiologic research

[Catherine R Lesko](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term="Lesko%20CR"\[Author\])

## Abstract

An impactful epidemiologic question is one that, if answered, could inform meaningful action to reduce the burden of disease in the population it concerns. We propose a set of factors that could be used for discussing, evaluating and communicating the public health impact of epidemiologic studies. These factors pertain to the burden and distribution of disease, the potential for an intervention to alter the disease burden, and the context in which the study is conducted. The disease burden is characterized by the number of cases, severity or cost of disease, and distribution of disease across the population. The potential for intervention is characterized by the mutability of the exposure itself, the prevalence and distribution of other causes of the disease in the population, the prevalence of the exposure and risk of the outcome under the natural course (prior to any intervention), <sup>1</sup> and the feasibility of intervening. An epidemiologic question need not be impactful along all these factors to make answering it worthwhile. However, answering epidemiologic questions with more of these factors present will likely have greater public health impact than answering questions for which these factors are absent. We hope that collecting these factors into a single framework may aid students and senior epidemiologists alike when organizing arguments for the value of their own work or attempting to evaluate the impact of others’ work.

**Keywords:** Disease burden, Framework, Intervention, Impact, Public health, Significance

## Introduction

Epidemiology is “the study of the occurrence and distribution of health-related states or events in specified populations, including the study of the determinants influencing such states, and the application of this knowledge to control the health problems.” <sup>2</sup> Epidemiologic questions could be about description, “risk factor” investigation (conditional description), causal effect estimation, or prediction. <sup>3 – 6</sup> Regardless of the specific type of question, epidemiology is not a theoretical science; it is an applied science. Thus, a “good” epidemiologic study should aim to impact or influence population health. But how do we define public health impact?

Historically, commentaries have argued that epidemiologic impact was tied to the positioning of an exposure in the causal chain under study: <sup>7</sup> more distal, macro-level exposures were argued to be more “fundamental,” <sup>8 , 9</sup> while studies of micro-level causal relationships were argued to be more robust because they examined biological mechanisms. <sup>10</sup> We do not intend to rekindle this conflict. <sup>11</sup> Exposures and outcomes exist in interconnected pathways such that the exposure in one investigation might be the outcome in another, and vice versa. <sup>7 , 12</sup> For example ( Figure 1 ), a macro-level epidemiologic analysis might describe how ambient heat affects sleep quality; <sup>13</sup> a meso-level epidemiologic analysis might describe how sleep quality affects inflammatory biomarkers; <sup>14</sup> and a micro-level epidemiologic analysis might describe how inflammatory biomarkers affect cardiovascular disease. <sup>15</sup> The specific taxonomy of questions into levels is not the point. Rather, we reiterate that research at all of the levels can be impactful, <sup>7 , 11</sup> but the impact is enhanced by understanding the causal pathway of interest and intersecting pathways (e.g., noise pollution or cognitive function in Figure 1 ). <sup>9 , 12</sup>

### Figure 1.

Open in a new tab

An example of epidemiologic research questions across multiple levels with one simple strand of a broader causal web (ambient heat affects other meso-level and micro-level variables and cardiovascular disease has other macro-level and meso-level causes)

### Influences on impact

Rather than debate what types of topics are valuable for epidemiologists to study, we aim to articulate a framework for discussing, evaluating and communicating the public health significance of epidemiologic studies—this could be single studies or areas of study. The framework we present is not intended to exclude any epidemiologic research as “not impactful.” Some of the considerations we present may seem self-evident and others are not new, having previously been discussed individually in the literature. However, we hope that collecting them all here may serve as an aid to students and senior epidemiologists alike when organizing arguments for the value of their own work or attempting to evaluate the impact of others’ work (e.g., in peer-reviewing papers or grant applications).

Broadly, we define public health impact as the potential we have to alter the burden of disease (i.e., the absolute number of cases or their effects on quality of life) in a population. This potential is bounded by: the burden and distribution of that disease in the population as it exists in the real world (the natural course), our ability to shift the burden of disease, and research context ( Box 1 ). An epidemiologic question may derive its impact by improving on prior studies in a single domain. However, an epidemiologic question that touches on multiple factors may have greater impact overall.

#### Box 1. Factors influencing public health impact of an epidemiologic study.

Defining the burden of disease and distribution of the outcome

* 1
  
  Absolute burden of disease in the population (number of cases)
* 2
  
  Disease severity or “cost” of the outcome
* 3
  
  Distribution of disease across the population

Defining the potential for intervention

* 4
  
  Mutability of the exposure
* 5
  
  Prevalence and distribution of component causes of disease in the population
* 6
  
  The natural course
* 7
  
  Feasibility of the intervention

Research in context

* 8
  
  Novelty and innovation

#### Burden of disease

Epidemiologic studies may have greater impact by studying diseases that are more common, more severe, or more heterogeneously distributed (relative to studying diseases that are rare, benign, or uniformly distributed). That is, the amount of disease and suffering that could be prevented or mitigated is bounded by the amount of disease and suffering that exists in the world, and the potential disease that could be averted is related to the distribution of disease within and across populations.

##### 1\. Absolute burden of disease

“Absolute” burden of disease can be communicated as a measure of disease frequency derived from the number of cases of disease. <sup>16 , 17</sup> Although disease risk may be low overall (e.g., lung cancer), it may be high in subpopulations (e.g., in miners). Thus, when quantifying absolute burden, the target population of interest should be explicitly specified according to person, place, and time. <sup>18 , 19</sup> Accurately quantifying the burden of disease is challenging and may itself be impactful, <sup>3 , 18</sup> for example, by drawing attention to an understudied disease or ensuring that adequate public health and clinical resources are available to diagnose, treat, or manage the disease.

##### 2\. Disease severity or cost

Intuitively, we understand that some health outcomes are “worse” than others. For example, an injury resulting in death is worse than an injury requiring an outpatient encounter. One could “artificially” inflate estimates of the population burden of injuries by expanding the definition of an event to encompass a broader range of injury severity – thus counting more outcomes – but the average severity and cost of the outcomes would be lower. <sup>20</sup>

Epidemiologic studies may be impactful by quantifying the severity and cost of different outcomes, e.g., quality of life, survival after disease onset, or monetary costs. Alternatives that are less common but useful for quantifying disease severity include utility functions <sup>21 , 22</sup> and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs). DALYs estimate the gap between life expectancy in the ideal health state without a particular disease/health condition and the current health state, accounting for both mortality and morbidity of a disease. <sup>23 – 25</sup> Although quantifying the burden of disease to the precision of a DALY is not the only option to evaluate the public health impact of a particular epidemiologic question, the principles behind their estimation illustrate how one might weigh the number of outcomes (where less severe outcomes will be more common than more severe outcomes) against the utility value, cost, or reduced quality of life associated with each outcome.

Epidemiologic studies might also be impactful by identifying potential interventions on disease severity. While primary prevention seeks to prevent disease from occurring in the first place, secondary and tertiary prevention aim to detect disease in a preclinical phase to prevent progression and to treat disease early to reduce its severity—that is, disease burden can by impacted without reducing the absolute number of cases of disease by reducing the suffering, morbidity, or mortality associated with disease.

##### 3\. Distribution of disease

Understanding the distribution of disease in a population has implications for our ability to reduce the burden of disease. A heterogeneous distribution of disease in a population may imply the existence of modifiable risk factors for disease <sup>26</sup> and may prompt additional epidemiologic studies into interventions on those risk factors. That is, a heterogeneous distribution of disease might have implications for our ability to affect the _absolute_ burden of disease.

A disease may also correlate with nonmodifiable characteristics such as demographics or membership in particular social groups. There are at least three potential implications for such findings: one might use the findings to attempt to target accepted prevention interventions at groups with high risk or prevalence of disease as a matter of efficiency, one might search for explanations for the differential distribution of disease, or one might seek to intervene on the differential distribution of disease because the difference is considered unfair or _inequitable_ . Labeling a difference in the distribution of disease a _disparity_ requires an understanding about the causes of disease and value judgements about their distribution in the population. <sup>27 , 28</sup> Identifying and monitoring a health disparity can be impactful as a call to action to reduce the disparity; because the correlate of the disparity is not itself a modifiable risk factor, determining the action(s) that would reduce the disparity is a distinct, non-trivial task that is also potentially impactful.

#### Potential for intervention

Once we have determined that there is more disease and/or more severe disease than we, as a society, consider acceptable, the next step is to think about how we might decrease disease burden.

##### 4\. Mutability of exposure or hypothesized intervention

There have been arguments among epidemiologists about whether an exposure need be modifiable for an effect estimate to be interpretable. <sup>29 – 33</sup> Estimating “the effect” (forgive imprecision of language <sup>29 , 31</sup> ) of exposures for which there is no yet-identified well-defined intervention may still have public health impact by improving our understanding of hypothesized causal pathways (sometimes called “etiology” although such claims from epidemiologic studies ought also be paired with strong biologic research) and stimulating research into possible interventions. Some potential exposures are clearly modifiable, but there is as-of-yet (i.e., at the time the research is conducted) not a well-defined intervention on the exposure. For example, an understanding of the role of HDL and LDL cholesterol in the development of cardiovascular disease was necessary to motivate research into treatments to modify lipid levels (tertiary prevention) and to confirm its potential as a surrogate outcome in primary prevention studies. <sup>34</sup> Some potential exposures are made modifiable by technological advances, e.g., genes and gene therapy. And some exposures are not modifiable but are proxies for things that are, e.g., race as a proxy for racism. There are problems with interpreting the measure of association from such studies as a measure of effect, and perhaps it is a misnomer to call the independent variable an “exposure” in that case. However, such studies (descriptions of associations, unadjusted and perhaps adjusted by other hypothesized sources of variation) <sup>16 , 33 , 35 , 36</sup> can be impactful by motivating action on the underlying causes of disease; as aptly stated by Robinson and Bailey, “in democratic nations, systemic change tends to be driven not by precise quantitative estimates but by narratives about causation, cultural values, and attribution of moral responsibility.” <sup>37</sup>

As our understanding of causal relationships deepens, estimating the effects of specific _interventions_ to change the exposure distribution yields evidence about how currently available actions might _alter_ population health. Different interventions targeting the same exposure might have different effects, <sup>30</sup> both on the average individual and on the population burden of disease. <sup>38</sup> Many types of interventions are possible and ideally, decisions about the best intervention to pursue will be based on data; epidemiologic studies can be impactful by providing those data. Primary preventive interventions might act by removing the harmful exposure, altering people’s susceptibility to the exposure (e.g., by removing other necessary components of a sufficient causal set), <sup>39</sup> or altering the effect of the exposure on the outcome (e.g., harm-reduction interventions). <sup>40</sup> Interventions that aim to change the exposure effect might also change the prevalence of exposure, and estimates of effect should consider the full spectrum of outcomes. For example, the installation of bike lanes to decrease risk of bike-car collisions per mile ridden might increase the number of miles ridden as people perceive biking to be safer, paradoxically increasing the absolute number of bike-car collisions. <sup>41</sup> Secondary preventive (e.g., screening) interventions might result in an (possibly temporary) increase in the number of cases of disease (some of which may be false positives who suffer individual-level harms from confirmatory testing) but hopefully reduced disease severity and reduced disease-specific mortality. <sup>42</sup> Finally, tertiary preventive interventions should not directly impact the incidence of disease (although they may do so indirectly, as in the case of antiretroviral therapy for people with HIV who, when they have an undetectable viral load are unable to transmit their infection) <sup>43</sup> but they may ideally, actually, increase the prevalence of disease by allowing people to live longer. Tertiary preventive interventions should also ideally improve quality of life or the utility function for people living with the disease in question. Often, new treatments are associated with no or marginal increases in survival but worse quality of life or increased risk of adverse events; <sup>44</sup> an honest evaluation of such interventions incorporates information on benefits and harms. <sup>45 , 46</sup>

##### 5\. Prevalence of other causes of disease

Assuming an exposure is amenable to intervention, the impact of changing the exposure is limited by the number and prevalence of other causes of the outcome in the population. <sup>39 , 47</sup> If the exposure of interest and the other cause(s) are both sufficient causes, the presence of either is enough to lead to development of disease. <sup>39 , 48</sup> Stated differently, when there is a high prevalence of other sufficient causes of the outcome, the proportion of the population with a “doomed” causal type—people who will get the outcome whether or not they are exposed <sup>47</sup> —will also be high. As the proportion of the population who are doomed increases, the proportion of the population with a “causal” causal type—people who will only get the outcome if exposed and whose disease could possibly be prevented—is constrained.

This constraint becomes more severe when exposures are correlated. Unfortunately, just as diseases are often distributed unevenly and inequitably across a population, so too are harmful _exposures_ . Therefore, attempts to lower the risk of disease by acting on one exposure at a time are likely to fail. Indeed, a key tenet of fundamental cause theory is that fundamental causes (e.g., racism) affect health outcomes through multiple, mutually reinforcing mechanisms, so intervening on a single mechanism (e.g., healthcare discrimination, financial redlining, over-policing, under-funding of public schools) will not substantially change the outcome risk. <sup>49 , 50</sup> In extreme cases, a harmful exposure might be perfectly correlated with other harmful factors such that, to affect any change in the risk of disease, one _must_ imagine joint interventions on multiple exposures or factors at once. <sup>51</sup>

This situation may be more common than is commonly appreciated: models that “control for” other causes of the outcome through stratification or standardization without considering joint effects <sup>52 , 53</sup> might find the primary exposure is independently associated with the outcome and miss the sub-additive interaction (antagonism) <sup>54</sup> between the primary exposure and other causes of the outcome that is the hallmark of this phenomenon. For example, in a study of women with HIV in clinical care in the US, we found sub-additive joint effects of alcohol misuse and marijuana use on risky sexual behavior: interventions to reduce alcohol use in women with both exposures would not have changed sexual risk behaviors if marijuana use persisted. <sup>55</sup> Another, broader approach to evaluating the complex relationships between several contributing and interplaying exposures is multi-level modeling of various exposures on multiple levels (individual, system, community, etc.) or simulation-based models. <sup>9 , 56</sup> However, as we move toward modeling intervention effects and multi-level outcomes of the joint effects of multiple exposures, the number of assumptions on which our conclusions rely increase dramatically, and results should be interpreted cautiously.

##### 6\. The natural course

Most studies that aim to estimate causal effects estimate “exposure effects.” The average causal risk difference (i.e., the difference in risk if everyone was exposed vs. no one was exposed) is the canonical exposure effect and has the nice feature of being interpretable as the average individual risk difference for a member of the target population in which it was estimated. <sup>57</sup> In contrast, “intervention effects” contrast of the observed risk in the absence of an intervention (the natural course) with the expected risk under some shift in the distribution of the exposure of interest brought about by a well-defined intervention. Intervention effects quantify the burden of disease potentially averted. <sup>38 , 58 , 59</sup> Because outcomes averted is anchored to the natural course—the burden of disease absent any intervention <sup>1 , 36</sup> —describing the burden and distribution of disease in a population is itself impactful. <sup>3 , 18</sup> An additional feature of intervention effects is that the risk under the natural course incorporates the prevalence of the exposure in the target population: the observed risk is a weighted average of the risk among people with “doomed” and “immune” causal types (whose risk will not be altered by the intervention); people with a “causal” (or “preventive”) causal type who were not exposed (and whose risk will not be altered by an intervention to remove exposure); and people with a “causal” (or “preventive”) causal type _who were exposed_ (who are the only people whose outcomes could be shifted by an intervention to remove exposure). Being explicit: the upper limit on the number of outcomes that could be averted with a perfect intervention to eliminate a harmful exposure (ignoring momentarily whether such an intervention exists) is necessarily the number of disease outcomes among people who experienced the harmful exposure under the natural course.

If the exposure that is the target of an intervention is rare, reducing or eliminating the exposure will not prevent a substantial number of disease outcomes, regardless of the exposure effect. <sup>60</sup> Although this principle has been discussed around the calculation of population attributable fractions, <sup>61 , 62</sup> calculating population attributable fractions requires additional assumptions not required for intervention effects. Intervention effects have the additional benefit of being more easily understood and translated into policy and thus public health impact. <sup>63</sup> Additionally, when using the natural course as the reference condition, the bounds on impact related to prevalence of exposure and prevalence of other risk factors for the outcome will be more clearly communicated in the form of a smaller intervention effect estimate and the estimand could be plugged directly into a cost-effectiveness analysis (e.g., to calculate cost per case of disease averted). <sup>58 , 60 , 64</sup>

Whether one is estimating exposure or intervention effects, the potential impact of a study can be enhanced by estimating effects on the absolute scale instead of or in addition to the relative scale. A “large” relative measure of effect on a rare disease might distract us from an intervention that has a “small” relative effect on a common disease, but a greater impact in absolute terms.

##### 7\. Feasibility of the intervention

Interventions cannot be implemented without resources (e.g., financial, human, political) and effort (e.g., by systems, clinicians, public health officials, or the individuals at risk for disease), and different interventions may require very different levels of each. Resources and effort required to implement an intervention may also depend on how it is delivered (e.g., universally or targeted to high-risk populations). Interventions that reach broader segments of society and require less individual effort (e.g., policies that change the environment in which people make decisions) may be more efficient than interventions that focus on modifying individual behavior. <sup>65</sup> However, that efficiency depends on the prevalence and distribution of the exposure the intervention is targeting. When exposures are less common but perhaps more strongly associated with a negative outcome, or when the most effective intervention is resource-intensive, it may be more efficient to target the intervention to high-risk groups.

Evaluating or understanding the feasibility or cost of an intervention may require cross-disciplinary collaborations, although epidemiologists are increasingly embracing the field of implementation science, which can aid in this process. Even if not evaluating feasibility or cost directly, epidemiologists have a responsibility to understand—at least in broad terms—the factors influencing feasibility and cost of the interventions they study. For example, who will bear the cost? The individuals who stand to benefit, or other individuals, governments/taxpayers, or producers of unhealthy products and environments (e.g., companies who are polluting)? Additionally, acceptability of the intervention depends on many things including the target population’s utility function for the outcome the intervention aims to reduce and how they prioritize prevention of the disease in question versus other outcomes; the cost of the intervention to the individual; and the degree to which the community has been involved in the research/the degree to which community values have been incorporated. The latter consideration also incorporates the quality of communication and level of trust between the community and the research team. Community partnerships can increase the probability that an intervention will improve public health.

Although studying cost-effective, feasible interventions might increase the public health impact of an epidemiologic study, there is also a balance to be struck. The cost and feasibility of interventions can change over time, potentially as a result of our research; political will and support for interventions might increase as a result of epidemiologic analyses showing the potential impact of such interventions. As such, we should not restrict ourselves to studying things based on the current climate.

##### 8\. Research context: novelty and innovation

The last factor influencing the impact of an epidemiologic study is how likely results are to shift the field or to challenge existing epidemiologic knowledge. <sup>66</sup> Much of this is related to what is known about a health outcome at the time the study is undertaken: the context. If one is reporting the seventeenth cohort study of the effect of exposure _A_ on outcome _Y_ in population _T_ , (in the absence of some other innovation in study design or bias control) the impact of that study is likely to be minimal relative to the first pragmatic trial of the effect of an intervention on _A_ , or the first cohort study that measured key confounder _Z_ , or the first study to appropriately apply an unbiased g-estimator to the study of an exposure with time-varying confounding affected by prior exposure. This point is in accordance with Bayesian reasoning <sup>67 , 68</sup> – what we learn (our posterior probability assigned to some scientific truth) is the product of what we knew (prior literature that formed our prior probability of the scientific truth) and what we see (in Bayes’ formula, the likelihood function from our study data). If many studies of the same exposure-outcome relationship have been conducted, the prior will be strong, and the posterior is unlikely to move substantially in response to new data. If there are few prior studies or if prior studies were subject to unaddressed systematic biases, the prior will be diffuse, and the data have more potential to influence the posterior and thus the anticipated public health impact.

Epidemiologic methods work can have public health impact by enhancing the novelty or innovation of study designs for answering a range of questions about different health outcomes or exposures. Improvements in epidemiologic methods have the potential to influence many different public health problems. The introduction of new study designs that rely on different assumptions than standard approaches can help test those assumptions and increase confidence in (or refute) existing research. <sup>69 , 70</sup> Innovation in epidemiologic methods or study design often emerges from collaboration with scientists from other fields and participation in multidisciplinary work.

### How do we measure public health impact?

To provide a concrete example of this impact evaluation, consider the relationship between HIV risk and male circumcision <sup>71</sup> (we provide prominent factors from Box 1 that apply to each line of epidemiologic inquiry). Descriptive studies consistently demonstrate the burden of HIV infection is higher in sub-Saharan Africa than in other geographic regions, in terms of both the number of cases () and the severity and cost of disease () related to access to medical care and treatment. However, investigations into the distribution of disease also showed heterogeneity in HIV infection rates by religious affiliation, with Muslim communities having lower rates of HIV (). This was hypothesized to be related to the prevalence of circumcision among Muslim men, and circumcision was subsequently investigated as a possible modifiable exposure for HIV transmission (, #6). Trials (, #8) later confirmed that providing male circumcision resulted in fewer HIV transmissions. <sup>72</sup> However, subsequent epidemiologic research also showed the importance of promoting abstinence for 6 weeks after surgery because HIV transmission risk was increased until healing was complete (). <sup>73</sup> Each of the studies—from initial descriptions of HIV burden to later observational studies and randomized trials related to circumcision as an intervention—thus displayed at least one factor demonstrating impact and informed meaningful public health action. Future studies will be required to continue to monitor HIV incidence, key populations and barriers to prevention, and the effects of interventions aimed at HIV treatment and prevention.

## Discussion

To link to existing literature and following convention, we focused on questions aimed at reducing the burden of disease. <sup>74</sup> Similar considerations would apply to studies aimed at increasing the prevalence and quality of health. <sup>75 – 77</sup> Many types of questions could inform meaningful action to increase the prevalence of “good” health or to reduce the burden of disease in a population. The need for action and the types of actions we choose might differ depending on whether our aim is to improve health or to reduce disease. <sup>74 , 75</sup>

It has been suggested that asking a well-defined question is in conflict with asking an “impactful” question. <sup>56 , 78</sup> For example, refining a question to make it “well-defined” <sup>79 – 81</sup> may limit the people/places/scenarios to which one can make inference. However, results cannot be impactful if the question is so broad as to be unanswerable. Similarly, one might ask a theoretically impactful, well-defined question that is not answerable with existing data. This could, perhaps, prompt the collection of new data or prompt the researcher to revise the question such that by combining available data with additional assumptions, one could obtain at least a preliminary answer to the question. If too many untenable assumptions are required to map the available data to the estimand, even if a _question_ is impactful, there may be too much uncertainty to believe the results. This balance is reflected in the difference between the “latent”(what researchers hope will come of the results) versus “stated” (what the study will deliver) research objective. <sup>66</sup>

We have provided a framework for describing the potential impact of an epidemiologic question anchored to the magnitude of the health problem it seeks to address—in terms of number, severity, and distribution of cases; the potential to shift the health problem—in terms of the nature of the intervention and prevalence of exposure and other component causes; and research context. We hope it will be helpful for researchers as they frame their questions and communicate the public health importance of their work.
